title: Spinning Romney wool into DK-weight 2-ply
subtitle: Short-forward draw on a longwool breed for a smooth, hard-wearing yarn
excerpt: Romney is a long-staple longwool with low crimp and staples of 15-20 cm. Short-forward draw keeps the long fibres controlled and produces a smooth, semi-worsted singles. Two singles plied together at around 12 WPI give a DK-weight yarn well suited to outerwear and accessories.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. The short-forward draw technique on longwool breeds follows the conventional teaching set out in Alden Amos, The Alden Amos Big Book of Handspinning (referenced for substance, not quoted). Romney's behaviour at the wheel is well documented across British spinning guilds and commercial top suppliers.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of a single lock of raw or prepared wool from cut end to tip. Staple length determines which drafting technique suits the fibre: short staples (under 8 cm) work well with a woollen long-draw; long staples (15 cm and over) are easier to control with a short-forward draw because the drafter's hands stay closer together than the staple is long.

A group of British sheep breeds characterised by long staples, a lustrous sheen, and low crimp. Romney, Bluefaced Leicester, Border Leicester, Wensleydale, and Lincoln are all longwools. Longwool yarns have less stretch than fine-wool yarns and suit hard-wearing projects such as outerwear, bags, and rugs.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Tie hair back, roll sleeves, no necklaces near the drive band, flyer, or bobbin. Loose fibres caught in spinning gear stop the wheel.

content.text: Romney top is a good wheel-spinning fibre for spinners who have mastered basic short-draw on finer wools and want to understand how breed changes the drafting. Romney is a
longwool
with a
staple length

of 15-20 cm and low crimp. Those two properties together mean a woollen long-draw does not suit it: the long, parallel fibres slide rather than bunch, and the resulting singles is uneven. Short-forward draw keeps one hand always within the staple length, controlling how much fibre enters the twist at each pull. Aim for a singles at around 24 WPI that plies to a 2-ply at approximately 12 WPI, consistent with DK weight.
What you need

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony)
items.qty: 1, set to a twist ratio of 8:1

content.text: Preparing the fibre

Romney top arrives combed: the fibres run in parallel along the length of the top. Draft a length of about 30 cm off the end and split it lengthways into two or three narrower strips if it feels dense. Spinning from a narrower strip gives better sensitivity over the drafting triangle. Do not pre-draft more than a metre at a time or the fibre loses the alignment that makes it easy to control.

Check your wheel is on a mid-range whorl that gives a twist ratio of about 8 twists per inch on the singles. Set the brake so the take-up is gentle, with a slight tug needed to advance the yarn onto the bobbin. Too much take-up pulls the yarn before enough twist has built, producing thin spots.
Spinning
Step 1: Start the singles

Join onto the leader using a short overlap, then hold the top in your back hand and let the front hand act as the pinch gate. Treadle steadily to build twist in the leader before drawing back. The short-forward draw works like this: the front (pinch) hand holds a few centimetres ahead of the drafting triangle, the back hand draws backward by 10-12 cm, then the front hand releases and advances, allowing the waiting twist to run into the drafted zone. The movement is a pull-and-release, not a constant slide.

Spin Z-twist for the singles (wheel treadled so the yarn twists clockwise when viewed end-on). Keep a consistent treadling speed. If the singles thickens, slow the draw; if it thins or breaks, speed up the draw slightly or reduce treadle rate.
Step 2: Check the twist as you go

Aim for 8 twists per inch on the singles. To count: stop treadling, hold the yarn near the orifice with one hand, and count the spirals over 2.5 cm (1 inch) of yarn. Eight spirals is the count to hold for DK-weight Romney. Fewer than six will produce an under-twisted singles that drifts apart in plying. More than ten produces a wiry, over-twisted singles that kinks back on itself when you let it relax.

When the first bobbin is full, fill a second bobbin at the same settings. Consistency between the two bobbins is more important than hitting a particular twist count exactly: if both singles are slightly under-twisted by the same amount, a balanced ply is still achievable.
Plying and setting the twist

Place the two bobbins on a lazy kate behind or beside the wheel. Ply with S-twist (treadle the opposite direction from spinning) at a lower twist ratio, around 5-6 twists per inch on the ply. Hold both singles in the back hand, keeping light tension, and let the plying twist marry the two strands evenly. Check balance as you go: let a short length hang free from your hand. A balanced 2-ply hangs straight. If it corkscrews clockwise (Z), it is under-plied; if it corkscrews anti-clockwise (S), it is over-plied.

Wind the plied yarn onto the niddy-noddy and tie the hank loosely in three places with waste yarn. Soak the hank in lukewarm water for 20 minutes. Lift out, press gently in a towel to remove water, and snap the hank once or twice between your hands to even the twist. Hang under no tension, or with a very light weight, to dry. Romney dries relatively quickly because it has low crimp and does not hold water as tenaciously as fine wools.

Measure WPI after drying: wrap the yarn around a ruler or WPI gauge for 2.5 cm (1 inch) without overlapping or stretching. A DK-weight 2-ply sits at approximately 12 WPI. If it measures closer to 14-15 WPI, the singles were finer than intended; re-calibrate the twist ratio upward on the next batch to spin more yardage from the same weight of top.
Common mistakes

items.cause: Too much take-up (the wheel pulling the yarn onto the bobbin faster than twist builds), or the pinch hand releasing too early before enough twist enters the drafted zone.
items.fix: Reduce the drive-band tension to lighten take-up. Keep the front (pinch) hand closed for a full beat of treadling before releasing. If breaks continue at the same point each time, look for a knot or thin spot in the top: pull that section out and overlap fresh fibre.

items.problem: The plied yarn corkscrews badly even after soaking
items.cause: Singles over-twisted (too many twists per inch) combined with insufficient ply twist, or vice versa.
items.fix: Let the hank re-soak in slightly warmer water and snap it more firmly. If the corkscrew persists after drying, wet-finish with a brisk snap while the yarn is still hot from the water: this resets Romney's low-crimp fibres more than it would a crimpy fine wool. For the next batch, aim for 8 tpi on the singles and 5-6 tpi on the ply.

items.problem: The finished yarn is noticeably hairy rather than smooth
items.cause: Some fibre escaped the drafting triangle and was caught by the twist at an angle, rather than lying parallel along the yarn.
items.fix: This is partly characteristic of longwool: some halo is normal. Reduce it by keeping the drafting zone shorter (shorter draws, closer hand position) and by combing the top again with a fine-toothed comb if it arrived with any nep or crimp disruption.
